title: 2024/11/07 - Petitions
displayTitle: 2024-11-07 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Carjam Online Ltd: Reverse plans for the NZTA to charge for free stolen status data in October; Petition of Angela Clifford: Government
topic: Petition of Carjam Online Ltd: Reverse plans for the NZTA to charge for free stolen status data in October; Petition of Angela Clifford: Government
description: 7 November 2024 | Petitions Committee Petition of Carjam Online Ltd: Reverse plans for the NZTA to charge for free stolen status data in October; Petition of Angela Clifford: Government facilitates development of a national food strategy for Aotearoa Submitters Petition of Carjam Online Ltd: Reverse plans for the NZTA to charge for free stolen status data in October Carjam Online Limited - Paul Osborne, CEO Waka Kotahi - Chris Rodley, National Manager, Regulatory System Design - Meredith Ussher, Group General Counsel Petition of Angela Clifford: Government facilitates development of a national food strategy for Aotearoa Angela Clifford Ministry for Primary Industries: - Sarah Reader, Manager Food Policy Trans-Tasman Ministry of Health: - Michelle Mako, Director, Māori Public Health | Hauora Maori Tumatanu
descriptionSummary: Petition of Carjam Online Ltd: Reverse plans for the NZTA to charge for free stolen status data in October; Petition of Angela Clifford: Government. Petition of Carjam Online Ltd: Reverse plans for the NZTA to charge for free stolen status data in October; Petition of Angela Clifford: Government...

chunks.text: Yes. Welcome Paul. Oh, hey Paul. Now just Paul. And for the sake of those in the room, we'll just introduce ourselves. Um, Connor, I'm the chair of the committee. Greg Fleming from Good mp. Got Benjamin Ho. I live in Hamilton. Right. Okay, Paul. So you can take your petition as read.

We've got you for 10 minutes. Um, but be good to leave some time for questions at the end. Yeah, Totally. Uh, well thanks for allowing the opportunity to present this oral submission on behalf of Car Jam. Um, rather than, I think as you, as you wish, rather than wading through the detail of the existing submission, uh, what I'd like to do is focus on addressing some of the points in the N zts submission sort of bit by bit, if that's okay.

So, um, I dunno if you have the nzt a submission in front of you, but I'll read out the bits that I wanna discuss anyway. Um, so the first part that I'd like to talk about is, uh, a sentence in it that says, um, this was the first comprehensive review of fees and charges that N ZT a had undertaken since the agency was established in 2008, that this is not quite correct.

Uh, in 2011, uh, the existing data service at that time was subject to review. Uh, at that point, the NZTO was 6 million in profit, uh, in this area of their business, and they wanted to rejig things in line with legislation where they were only allowed to do cost recovery.

So yeah, in 2011, there was a review and they removed a lot of the more extensive data that was available to us, and that we were simply turning over to public for free at that time. Uh, there is another reason I'll mention this 2011 review, uh, that I'll come back to later and I wanna make sure that's understood that there's been multiple reviews.

Um, so the second point I'd like to address is the statement that Car Jam is of the view that the public cannot rely on the police data from the police website as it is not updated in real time, like the MVR, the Motor Vehicle Register. So that's the NZTA agreeing with the primary thrust of our argument that the police data is not as accurate or as timely as the NZTA.

And, uh, I don't know if you've really looked in, in absolute detail at this, but it's kind of non-intuitive that the NZTA data is more accurate than the police data that's published. So that's really a core part of our submission. It's a very important part that the NZTA data that they are now charging for is actually more accurate than the police data that they're suggesting that we use.

So it's very important to, to understand that it is not real time the police data, uh, and more importantly, the police list is incomplete. We provided the NZPA with a list of plates that we've discovered by manual searching that are in the NZTA Motor Vehicle register and are not listed as stolen in the police data.

Uh, there are many, many more of these kind of plates in the NZPA data that we have not had. Uh, we, we've not invested the significant cost to go through vast numbers of plates to find the absolute percentage, but there are many, just on a manual examination, I was able to provide the NZA with about 20 plates that are not in the police website data and are in the NZTA data.

This is, again, I'll repeat it. This is a core issue for Car Jam in wanting to make this, uh, reverse of the change that happened. The police data is not as accurate as the NZDA data. Uh, and as I say, it's quite non-intuitive and the differences between the, the two data sets we have never been able to fully determine.

We've had many, many conversations with the NZTA and with the police trying to determine how this happens, that the NZTA data appears to be more extensive and more real time than the police data, but never received any kind of answer explanation from anyone involved in this. So the third point I'd like to cover, uh, is the NZC a's statement about the costs that it's approximately 11 million per annum.

Uh, and the point of their changes were to introduce a per request charge, which they believe, uh, was a simple and fair way of making sure costs are recovered from the right parties. Um, the main point here to understand is this was not balanced against the risk, uh, the, the safety and the cost to the public and the police, and to some extent to industry, uh, in the fact that the NZTA data is better than the police data, and we can no longer provide that more accurate data to the public for free.

Um, and there is no other source of this free NZTA date, NZTA themselves do not published this freely to, uh, the public. So CAR Jam is viewed as the main source of vehicle information in New Zealand. It's not me bragging, this is just a general impression of people in New Zealand that CAR Jam is some kind of combination of the government, the police, and the NZTA.

They, we have many, many emails asking us about things saying why we don't change things. And it's, it's because we are not the government, we're not the police, we're not the NZTA, but we are viewed as a primary source of data and we can no longer give out that data about the stolen face of the car for free. Um, which is why we want this decision reversed.

Um, if the police website was real time and included all the vehicles that are in the NZTA motor vehicle register list is stolen. Uh, we wouldn't be here today. We, we wouldn't have an argument against these changes. Uh, but it is not, that's why we've spent many months and now comes this hearing, um, to try and get this decision reversed so we can provide the free data regarding the stolen status to the public, uh, to improve the security and reduce crime and all the other issues that I, I outlined in the original written submission.

Um, so the fourth point I wanted to talk about is that, uh, in the NZT a's written submission, they sides a general concern, uh, amongst people consulted in the, uh, consultation period about the additional costs that would have on businesses.

Kajam have never commented on this, both the change in 2011 that I mentioned before, and the change in 2023, uh, do not impact car jam's fundamental bottom line, uh, in any negative way. All it does is force us back an old school model of buying and selling data. The negative effect is that we can't give the free safety stolen information to the public.

That's the biggest negative effect out of, it's not any impact on car Jam's revenue or profit. Um, so we did not complain about the effect on our business. We were only complaining about the effect on the safety of the public. Um, so, uh, yeah, I think that's, uh, probably everything I wanted to say.

Uh, you guys have any questions or? I have not made anything clear. Yeah, can I just clarify, how do you get your revenue from what you do? The stolen status is just something we give out to the public for free all the time. And we do hundreds of thousands, actually, millions of queries per month. That's just how things are. So we are perceived as the primary source of information, uh, in New Zealand for vehicle data. And that's what's been taken away. The ability to deliver the most secure, the safest, the most accurate information about the stolen safe of a car for free to the public. That's what we want reversed.

We think this could be advantageous because it's not a big overwhelming, strategically complicated step. Um, a food security council for New Zealand could coordinate and integrate government and civil society response to food systems, shocks and tensions. Um, and we know that there is a, uh, cross agency working party within the government currently that's looking at a food strategy. So this wouldn't be reinventing the wheel. Alright. Thank you. Um, questions.
